fr:title.text: Putting the engineer in my engineering degree

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Putting the engineer in my engineering degree

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here I will be learning not only how a computer actually works, but how to actually leverage this knowledge to write more performant code. Along the way, I also intend to learn good coding practice and become all around a more capable engineer to differentiate myself from the hardware unaware.
I will also be keeping track of miscellaneous notes here that I deem relevant and things I want to look at in the future.
Notes on the book Inside the Machine by Jon Stokes.
For speed, we introduce the first notion of NUMA i.e. registers. Here the registers or stored in the register file, which is located very close to the ALUs.

Due to limited surface area of the CPU, we can only have so many registers. This necessitates the introduction of main memory or RAM. Data is transferred from main memory to the CPU via the memory bus.
This leads to the logical classification of instructions into two categories: arithmetic operations and memory operations.

To ease programmability, there are general purpose registers that function as both integer values and correspond to memory addresses. This is useful for register-relative addressing, where we can offset all memory accesses by some register value.
To support both values and registers, we need to distinguish as ''immediate types'' vs. ''register types''. We will see why when we actually make a instruction set that must correspond to binary.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch instruction from memory

html:ul.html:li: This entails reading the instruction and loading it into the instruction register.
Also we increment the program counter.

html:ol.html:li: Decode instruction

html:ul.html:li: This entails extracting the opcode from the instruction register.
We also extract the operands from the instruction register.
We also extract the destination from the instruction register.

html:ol.html:li: Execute instruction

html:ul.html:li: If the instruction is an arithmetic operation, we use the ALU.
If the instruction is a memory operation, we use the load/store unit.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: These instructions do not take arbirary amounts of time to execute. They depend on the clock cycle. This is inputted via the motherboard and determines when to read the values stored in the current state.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Branch Instructions: There are two types conditional and unconditional.

html:ol.html:li: Unconditional: The program counter is set to the destination address.
Conditional: The program counter is set to the destination address if the condition is true.

html:ul.html:li: To support conditional branching, normally need to have some other sort of hardware. In the example given in this chapter, they keep a processor status word register that stores the output of the previous arithmetic instruction.
Many different types of conditional branching instructions based on the value in the PSW.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Branch instructions are added as a third case in the execute phase i.e.

html:ul.html:li: If the instruction is a branch instruction, check the condition and branch.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If the program uses relative addressing, then the OS must load the base register of the code block to add to the branch destination register.
How does a computer boot: If a computer must fetch instructions, then what does it do at the beginning when there is no program running?

When you power on a computer, it is hard-wired to fetch an initial instruction from a pre-determined address. This memory address is also hardcoded and unchanged and contains the code for the BIOS. The BIOS is then responsible for making sure everything is working correctly before jump to the bootloader, which then loads the OS.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Previously, we discussed the fetch-execute loop. However, it is actually the case that the store / write part of execute is treated as a different stage, yielding

html:ol.html:li: Fetch instruction from memory
Decode instruction
Execute instruction

html:ul.html:li: Now a read / add

html:ol.html:li: Write result to memory

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Each of these represents one phase in the pipeline. However, these by no means take the same amount of time.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We have previously been determining the clock cycle as the time to complete an entire instruction i.e. to complete the whole pipeline. However, with overlapping instructions we now can shrink the clock cycle to the time it takes to complete the slowest phase of the pipeline. Dividing the pipeline into roughly equal stages is important to ensure that no one stage is idle during the majority of the cycle.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Pipelining does not increase the speed of execution of a single instruction. However, it does increase the program execution time. Taking this to the limit, we would want an extremely fine pipeline i.e. large depth, so that no stage is ever idle. Explicitly, for n stages a full pipeline will complete n instructions every cycle. However, there is a tradeoff due to the clock cycle constraints as deepening your pipeline can cause longer per-instruction execution time.

html:ol.html:li: Completion rate: number of instructions completed per nanosecond.

html:ul.html:li: Program execution time is \frac {\text {number of instructions per program}}{\text {avg completion rate per instruction}}

html:ol.html:li: Instruction throughput: number of instructions processor finishes executing per clock cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each metric also has the maximum theoretically achieved and the average.

The main thing preventing us from achieving our maximum theoretical throughput is pipeline stalls. Namely, for some reason we cannot execute a certain stage of the pipeline at a certain time i.e. we are waiting for a value to be loaded in from memory.

The instruction latency is the number of cycles it takes to complete an instruction. In a single cycle processor, this is 1. Or the minimal latency is the sum of the cycles for each stage of the pipeline. Though, this is hardly the true latency in practice.
With Moore's law and the unlocked capability of more transistors, each processor could have multiple ALUs on chip performing more than a single scalar operation. Hence the name superscalar.
To extend the fetch-execute loop, the decode stage becomes a decode / dispatch stage, where the processor must decide which instructions can be run in parallel.
An execution unit is any circuitry responsible for executing a single instruction. This could be a single ALU, a single load/store unit, or a combination of both.
Arithmetic operations are things like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Namely, operations that can be performed on any type of number
Logical operations are things like AND, OR, and NOT. Namely, operations that can be performed on integer values, or the contents of special registers like PSW.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: From now on, we will be using the term ALU to refer to exeucution unit for either arithmetic or logical operations i.e. float point units(FPU) OR integer logic units(ILU).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The load store unit is responsible for loading and storing data from memory. It is also responsible for performing the memory address calculations with a lightweight addition unit.
The branch execution unit is responsible for performing the branch instructions. In the simple case currently considered, it reads the PSW and decides whether to take the branch.
Superscalar processor still responsible for implementing the ISA. How the hardware implements an ISA is referred to as the microarchitecture.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: To actually perform this mapping, processor have a microcode engine that is a mini-CPU inside the CPU. It has small memory to hold the microcode programs and an execution unit to execute them. Its task is to translate the ISA instructions into machine code to run on the actual hardware. Intuitively, it is like an emulator.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Microcode engines take up space in the processor. Especially for CISC instruction sets which require larger microcode programs. Therefore, in RISC they got rid of the microcode engine and instead rely on compilers.

fr:title.text: Challenges to Pipelining and Superscalar Architecture

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Challenges to Pipelining and Superscalar Architecture

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Data hazard: Operation depends on the result of a previous operation.

html:ul.html:li: One trick to avoid this is using forwarding, where the result of a previous operation is used as the input to the next operation rather than waiting for it to complete storing the result.

Another trick is to use register renaming. The programming model often specifies fewer registers than available in hardware. Therefore, we can use the extra registers as temps to store intermediate computation
202643Example Say we have the following code: add r1, r2, r3 add r4, r5, r2 We can use the extra registers to store the intermediate result of the second addition and then write back to r2 after the first one completes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Structural hazard: when the processor does not have enough resources to execute an instruction.

html:ul.html:li: In the superscalar architecture, we need our register file to allow for simultaneous writes. Otherwise, we will not be able to execute simultaneous write instructions.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: To connect each register to each ALU, would require a riduculous amount of wiring. Therefore, we need to use a register file to connect the registers to the ALUs. The register file allows reading a single specified register for each read port and write to a specific register for each write port. So for an n ALU chip, you would need 2n read ports and n write ports.
The die space of a register file scales quadratically with number of ports.
They often use different register files for different execution units, so each register file only needs ports for its execution units. This also decreases access time due to decreasing the size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Control hazard: when the processor has to decide what instruction to fetch next at a branch instruction.

html:ul.html:li: To avoid stall, modern processor use branch prediction.
Another cause of slowdown is actually the instruction load latency. To avoid this, we can use instruction caching (TBD).

fr:title.text: Chapter 5: The Intel Pentium and Pentium Pro

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Chapter 5: The Intel Pentium and Pentium Pro

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Caches: To alleviate the latency of accessing main memory, processors have smaller (faster) memory located closer to the chip known as a cache.

html:ul.html:li: Caches are organized in a cache hierarchy. The first level is the L1 cache, which is the fastest cache. The second level is the L2 cache, which is slower than the L1 cache. The third level is the L3 cache, which is even slower than the L2 cache.
Furthermore, the L1 cache is normally split in half for data and instructions.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Branch Unit: The branch unit contains a branch execution unit and a branch prediction unit.

html:ul.html:li: To reduce stalling, we can perform speculative execution, where based on the prediction of the BPU, we execute the subsequent instructions. Then we only write back once we have confirmed the correct prediction of the branch.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: There is static and dynamic branch prediction, where the former is program-agnostic i.e. always predict the branch is taken. This is fine for certain loop-heavy programs but in general pretty bad.

The dynamic branch prediction makes use of the branch history table and the branch target buffer. The BHT stores how often previous branches are taken and then the BTB tells you which address those branches actually jump to in order to speculatively execute. Apparently, BTB misses are very costly.
The Pentium combines these by using static prediction if no entry is found and dynamic otherwise.
The modified pipeline for the Pentium is as follows:

html:ol.html:li: Fetch / Prefetch: fetch instruction from instruction cache.
Decode 1:.

html:ul.html:li: decode instruction to Pentium's internal format
branch prediction also happens here.
If instruction is not a simple instruction, then it is converted via the microcode ROM into microops to be performed by CPU.

html:ol.html:li: Execute: execute instruction.
Write: write result to memory.

html:ul.html:li: Two integer ALUs. Responsible for the address calculations.
One floating point ALU.
Register file is arranged in a stack.
Can arbitrarily access elements on the stack, but for floating point operations one register must be at top of stack.
Had instruction to swap with top of stack. On all modern x86 can be executed in 0 cycles. But still need to decode.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: An estimated 30% of the Pentium's transistors are used for legacy support, mainly going towards the microcode ROM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To support out of order execution while preserving the illusion of sequential execution, we must add a issue buffer and a completion buffer. The reorder buffer is where we store a larger set of instructions than just two, reordering them so that we can execute multiple conflict-free instructions in parallel. When an instruction is selected we say it is issued.

However, to ensure that we match the intent of the programmer, we must store the intermediate results of these instructions internally in registers not visible to the programmer. We then write these to the correct registers once all results are available. When the results of an instruction are written back we say it is committed.

The Pentium Pro has the reservation station to issue instructions. Three instructions can dispatch into the reservations station at any given cycle and up to five instructions can be issued to be executed by the reservation station

To commit instructions, the Pentium Pro has the reorder buffer. The reorder buffer must ensure that instructions are commited in program order. Hence why it is at both the front and back of the pipeline. The reorder buffer keeps track of all relevant information i.e. instruction status, operands, register needs, position in program, etc.

html:ul.html:li: Also assists in register renaming.
Each of the 40 ROB entries has a data field that can be used as rename registers.
Speculated instructions are also added to the ROB but contain an additional indication that they are speculated. If the BPU mispredicts, they are cleared from the ROB.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: CISC vs. RISC: x86 is a CISC instruction set, meaning it contains more complex instructions. Namely, arithmetic instructions can be overloaded with memory locations directly instead of having to manually perform the loads and stores beforehand. Additionally, they allow support for complex types like strings.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The drawback is that this introduces a large processor burden i.e. we have to implement this functionality directly into the processor.

Once it became apparent that RISC was the future, Intel and AMD shifted to instruction translation. This means that they supported x86 by translating the CISC instructions to a more RISC-like instruction set internally supported by the processor. These are called micro-operations

Many CISC instructions translate to only one or two micro-ops. Therefore, they separate their decoders into simple and complex decoders, where the simple decoders are specific to just these operations.
All of this decoding / translation resulted in 40% of the Pentium pro's transistors being used for supporting backward compatibility......

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We now explore a RISC architecture in the line of PowerPC.
Standard 4-stage pipeline.
Instruction queue dispatching 3 instructions per cycle.

html:ul.html:li: Used primarily for branch detection.
Identifies unconditional branches or resolves conditional branches whose condition output is already available.
If taken, deletes branch instruction and loads instructions starting from branch location.
This is called branch folding.
Untaken branches "fall out" of the instruction queue, which is known as fall through.
No ROB equivalent. Static scheduling.
One integer ALU. Handles address calculations and regular arithmetic ops.
One floating point ALU.
For floating point dominant applications, integer ALU acts as address computer, accelerating pipeline.
Single cycle fused multiply-add.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Able to compete with two integer ALU architectures because simpler logic allowed 4x L1 cache (32kb vs. 8kb).

html:ul.html:li: Has dispatch bus only connect to BU, so that branch instructions do not take up dispatch bandwidth.
Can dispatch up to 2 non-branch instructions per cycle.
Adds commit unit and completion queue analogous to the commit buffer and ROB we have seen previously.
Dynamic scheduling + instruction window.
Added two execution units.

html:ol.html:li: System unit. For misc integer operations.
Load/store unit. For address calcluations.
Improved integer workload performance by decreasing the responsibility of integer ALU.

html:ul.html:li: FPU took significant hits.

html:ol.html:li: Can only execute three instructions every four cycles (mandatory bubble).
Double precision operations (including fmadd) take two cycles in execute stage -> one double precision multiply every two cycles.

html:ul.html:li: Splits decode / dispatch + writeback into two stages
Enables shorter clock cycles.
Can dispatch up to 6 instructions per cycle.
Dynamic branch prediction.
512 entry BHT with two bits per entry.
64 entry branch address cache equivalent to BTB.
Add Condition register unit.
Shares dispatch bus and other(?) resources with BU.
Two simple integer units and one complex integer unit.
Fully pipeline floating point unit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Frontend Pipeline:

html:ul.html:li: Instructions fetched from L1 instruction cache into instruction queue.
Folds branches that can be folded.
Dispatch up to 4 instructions from bottom of instruction queu.
During dispatch, rename registers and a ROB entry are assigned to each dispatched instruction.
An instruction cannot be dispatched if it needs an occupied execution unit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Issue phase:

html:ul.html:li: Each execution unit has own reservation station.
Within each reservation station, instructions are issued in order FIFO. But there is no guarantee across reservation stations.
Reservation stations are used so that instructions waiting on operands do not block following instructions that may be ready.
Four rules of dispatching:

html:ol.html:li: in-order rule: instructions are dispatched in order and are not executed out of order until they executed on their respective units.
execution unit availability: the execution unit's reservation station must have room. if it does not need to go to reservation station then the execution unit must be available.
completion buffer availability rule: must be room to add entry to ROB (completion buffer) so that we can ensure program order on commit.
register renaming rule: must be enough rename registers to temporarily restore results before committing..

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Completion phase: put instructions back in program order according to ROB entries.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
604 has small ROB and fewer reservation stations because it has a shallow pipeline, meaning that it can afford to stall. Tradeoff is lower clock speed.
ROB tracks all issued instructions.
Reservation stations hold all instructions waiting on operands.
Size must cover number of instructions simultaneously "in flight".
In flight instructions \approx depth \times width.
The 604e. In the 604e, they doubled the L1 cache size to 64kb and added a condition register unit. Freed up integer unit and branch unit from computing these conditional operations.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Same four stage pipeline as the 603/603e. Main differences:

html:ol.html:li: Two integer units vs. one.
Three cycle single precision floating point.
Six entry instruction queue, fetching up to 4 instructions per cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The smaller instruction window of 750 vs. 604 is not a problem because the pipeline is shorter as discussed previously.

The 750 has most of its performance gain from a clever trick in branch prediction. Namely, in traditional branch prediction, there is a branch history table (BHT) and branch target buffer (BTB). The branch target buffer just stores the address of the instruction to jump to if the branch is taken. However, it must still re-fetch this instruction to execute. The 750 uses a branch target instruction cache(BTIC) that stores the actual instruction to be executed on branch, avoiding re-fetching.
The main drawback of the 750 is that it did not support a SIMD instruction set.
The 7400 added a vector permute unit(VPU) and vector ALU(VALU) to support a SIMD instruction set. Also added

html:ul.html:li: Full double precision floating point unit.
8 entry instruction queue.

fr:title.text: Chapter 7: Pentium 4 vs. Motorola G4e Approaches and Considerations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Chapter 7: Pentium 4 vs. Motorola G4e Approaches and Considerations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In this chapter, they compare two designs: the Pentium 4 vs. the G4e.
The approach: wide and shallow. Optimizing for instruction level parallelism.
Seven stage pipeline.

html:ol.html:li: Additional second fetching stage.
Issue stage.

html:ul.html:li: Recall that instructions are issued in order. This can lead to stalls if none of the reservation stations / execution units are available.
To fix, they add general issue queue, vector issue queue and float issue queue, which can accept and issue 3,2, and 1 instructions per cycle respectively.
Issue queue differ from reservation station because it issues to a coarser group of execution units rather than per ALU i.e. GIQ issues to any of integer ALUs or SLU.

html:ol.html:li: Complete stage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The approach: narrow and deep. Optimizing for clock speed.
The calling card of the Pentium 4 is the incredibly deep pipeline. They have 20 stages, allowing incredibly fast clock speed (for marketing purposes).

html:ul.html:li: In order to keep fast backend fed, need very deep buffers (126 instructions) i.e. large instruction window.
Could not keep deep pipeline full because decoding was taking too long.
Decoding x86 requires breaking into micro-ops. Few cycles each time.
Instruction cache is now in L2 cache.
The trace cache is in L1 cache, and it stores already decoded micro-ops traces that are ready to be executed.
Trace cache fetch is after decode but is indexed directly during branch prediction.

The trace cache operates in two modes: execution mode and build mode. When there is a trace hit it feeds the trace directly to the execution logic. When there is a miss, it must fetch the instruction from the L2 cache and build the trace after decoding.
Trace cache allows storing speculative branches.
Normal instruction fetching stops fetching cache when a branch is detected.
Trace cache allows storing speculative micro-ops after branch to fully utilize 6 micro-op cache line.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Long (100s) of micro-ops instructions can pollute the trace cache.

Tag these long instructions. When we encounter a tag, the trace cache instead stores a pointer to the microcode ROM which then retrieves and dispatches the corresponding micro-ops without needing to store them in the cache.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: What is 64 bit computing? n-bit computing refers to how many bits a register can hold.

html:ul.html:li: 64 bit means they hold 64 bits.
64 bit instructions means the operands are 64 bits.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This means that the instruction size has not actually changed in size. The PC does change because the virtual address space has increased (we will see later).
Only affects integer and addressing units. Floating point and vector units retain same width > 64.

html:ul.html:li: Increased dynamic range i.e. representation went from 2^32 to 2^64.
Overflow or underflow is when the largest positive or negative number exceed the dynamic range.
64-bit address space.
x86-64 has 40-bit virtual address space, but idea is it allows efficient usage of much more physical ram.
One drawback of this is that pointers now take up twice as much cache space.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: It is not always beneficial to have 64-bit integers. If you do not need the extra range, then this is wasted cache space. Due to this, the default size of integers is typically 32 bits. To extend, you need to add a prefix (1 byte), which adds < 10% code size.
CPU clock cycle is decreasing much more rapidly than the clock cycle of the memory.
If you are a really fast factory, regardless of how big the truck is that is carrying your materials you must still wait the entire duration of the first delivery.
A cache is like having a nearby warehouse that you can quickly get more materials if you run out. Obviously, the bigger the better.

html:ul.html:li: Main memory
Access time: 10-60 ns.
Size: 64mb - 1gb.
Level 1 cache: smallest cache. Holds the most frequently used data.
Fast access time is due to being static ram.
Each cell has 4-6 transistors vs. 1 transistor for DRAM.
Access time: 2-8 ns.
Size: 8-128 kb.
Level 2 cache: drop off from L1 cache to main memory can be 50 stalled cycles, so we add a larger slower second level cache.
Access time: 5-12 ns.
Size: 0.5-8 mb.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A Bytes Journey Through Memory. First, we check the L1 cache if the corresponding cache line is not there it is a cache miss. Then, we check the L2 cache once again if the cache line is not there we have another cache miss. We then check main memory to see if the page is there. If the page is not there, then we have a page fault and must go to disk.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Cache misses: There are four types of cache misses.

html:ol.html:li: Compulsory miss: miss because data was never in the cache.
Conflict miss: miss because the data was evicted from the cache due to a conflict.
Coherence miss: miss because the data was invalidated due to a write to the cache from another core.
Capacity miss: miss because the cache was full and the data was evicted according to the eviction policy.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Spatial locality. if the CPU needs this data, then it will probably need nearby data in the future.

html:ul.html:li: We make use of spatial locality by using a cache line i.e. when some data is accessed, we load some fixed size surrounding data as well.
The sole data that we wanted is called the critical word.
RAM and caches are organized by these cache lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Temporal locality. if the CPU needs this data, then it will probably use it again in the future.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Temporal locality implies spatial locality but not vice versa. Namely, if nearby data is reused often, then it will still be kept by temporal locality.

The locality for code and data can be very different i.e. re-running same code with different data will have high temporal locality for code but not data. This is why we split the L1 cache into the instruction and data components.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The cache tag is a bit vector that is used to identify the cache line that the data is stored in.

Tag RAM: In the cache, there is a separate tag array that stores all of the tags of the resident data in the cache along with metadata regarding dirty, valid, coherent, etc. We separate into Tag RAM to make the check as fast as possible. Tag entries are small allowing a lot of reads in parallel.
Associativity: Once we know that a tag is present and valid, we need to find the corresponding data in the cache.

html:ul.html:li: Fully associative: any tag can go anywhere in the cache.
Direct mapped: every tag can only go to one specific location in the cache.
n-way set associative: divide cache into sets of size n. Each tag goes to one set n entries.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replacement policy: most common is least recently used. However, this takes too much time to compute in practice, so they often do pseudo-LRU (something like clock algorithm).
Block sizes: for best spatial locality, you want the largest block size possible. However, this has a few drawbacks

html:ul.html:li: Cache size: bringing large block requires evicting resident blocks, losing temporal locality.
More prone to cache pollution: most of the data you bring in will not be used.
Memory bandwidth: larger block size requires more bandwidth.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-through vs. Write-back: on a store, we have modified the data at a specific address. However, if we only update the cache, when it is evicted and we later try to load we will have the wrong value. Need to ensure main memory is coherent.

html:ul.html:li: Write-through: on every store, we write back to main memory.
Write-back: we only write back to main memory on eviction.
Need dirty bit to know if we need to write back.
Tradeoff: complextiy vs. less memory traffic.

fr:title.text: Intel's Pentium M, Core Duo, and Core 2 Duo

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Intel's Pentium M, Core Duo, and Core 2 Duo

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A processor often is asscoiated with three names. The brand name, the microarchitecture brand name, and the microarchitecture code name.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Clock speed scaling hit a power wall

html:ul.html:li: The power density is the number of watts disipated per unit area.
The dynamic power density is the amount of power dissipated per unit area due to switching of a chip's transistor
High clock speeds means more rapidly switching transistors.
You can also increase via cramming more transistors into same area.
The static power density is the amount of power dissipated per unit area due to leakage current.
Transistors leak more when they are smaller -> more transistors more leakage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Power wall necessitated low power design.
Fetch: Widened fetch buffer to 64 bytes to now decode up to 4 16-byte instructions at a time.

Decoding: Utilize micro-op fusion, where an x86 instruction that takes 2 micro-ops can be translated into a fused micro-op and sent to instruction queue. In the instruction queue, they can be serialized or executed in parallel.

html:ul.html:li: A store on x86 is two micro-ops: a store-address micro-op and store-data micro-op. Address micro-op can be sent to store-address execution unit, where as the store-data micro-op is sent to the store-data execution unit. Both located in the LSU. The Pentium M fuses these into a shared entry in the micro-op queue until they are issued.
A read-modify instruction the load micro-op and second micro-op are fused and tracked together. These must be executed serially.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Branch Prediction: They add a loop detector to identify loop exit conditions.

html:ul.html:li: Keep special counters to see how many times a loop iterates.
If loops iterate same number of times, can predict with 100% accuracy.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also have an indirect predictor.

html:ul.html:li: Works like branch history table but instead stores each branch's favorite target address.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Stack Execution Unit: x86 ISA has stack instructions like push and pop. They update dedicate stack pointer register ESP, traditionally carried out by dedicated micro-op.

html:ul.html:li: No longer use integer execution unit.
Dedicated stack execution unit with own adder to avoid using the integer ALU.
Never issue ESP related instructions to back-end.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Part of my revisiting CS fundamentals journey
Deliberate-Practice (1/66)

A process consists of a few primary components
Memory: each process requires its own portion of memory for (i) to store the instructions of the program to execute and (ii) for the memory required by the calling program. This is referred to as the address space.
Registers: for those familiar with computers, instructions typically deal with registers, where information is loaded from memory into registers to perform computation. Additionally, there are special registers reserved for certain mechanisms i.e.
Program Counter (PC): This register is in charge of telling us what instruction we are currently on in the executing program.
Stack and Frame Pointer: Used to manage where we currently are on the stack.
In order to implement a process, one must support each of the following operations

html:li.html:p: The operating system must implement a mechanism to create a process. This consists of loading the program from disk, initializing memory (i.e. stack and heap), and jumping to main (typically).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The stack is allocated up front and does not change. However, typically a small amount of memory is allocated for the heap and it grows via malloc. This is non-trivial because if malloc is called without available heap memory then memory must be allocated by the OS. I need to look into how this is done more.
The OS will also initialize the parameters to main i.e. argc or argv.

html:ol.html:li: Destroy: Clearly, if a process is misbehaved or the user just does not want it to run any longer, there must be some mechanism to kill the process.
Wait: In the case of dependencies (piping?), it may be useful to wait for a process to complete.
Miscellaneous Control: The example provided in the book is to suspend a process.
Status: It is helpful to be able to get information about running processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: There are also process states. These states are running, ready, and blocked. These states are pretty self-explanatory.
It seems throughout the book we will be seeing implementation examples from the smaller xv6 OS.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: In addition to the process implementation below, there is also a data structure that must keep track of all the processes. This is the process list or process control block.

html:ol.html:li:
Run process.py with the flags -l 5:100, 5:100. What should the CPU utilization be?
Kellen Kanarios2025623AnswerThe flags correspond to running two processes each with 5 instructions where each instruction has probability one of using the CPU. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu with this, we can conclude that the utilization is 100%. We will execute one instruction for the first process until it completes, then the five instructions of the next process. If both are waiting on IO, then I do not think this would be the case?
Kellen Kanarios2025623Solution Using the solution flags -cp, there is 100% utilization.

Run process.py with the flags -l 4:100, 1:0. How long does it take to complete both processes?
Kellen Kanarios2025623AnswerThe flags correspond to running one process with 4 instructions with each instruction having probability one of using the CPU and one process with one instruction that uses the IO. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 io io_done It will take 4 + \text {time of IO}. Since the process with 4 non-IO-dependent instructions will run each of those instructions and then the next process will begin which must wait for the entire duration of IO.
Kellen Kanarios2025623SolutionUsing the -cp flags it takes 11 timesteps to complete which is \approx 4 + \text {time of IO}.

Now switch the order of the processes: ./process-run.py -l 1:0,4:100. What happens now? Does switching the order mat- ter? Why?
Kellen Kanarios2025623Answer The output of the code is Process 0 io io_done
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu Depending on the time of IO (the -L flag), it should take \max (5, 1 + \text {time IO}). Switching the order does matter because we can run the CPU instructions while waiting for non-blocking IO. This motivates the idea of scheduling.
Kellen Kanarios2025623Solution It only takes 7 timesteps because we are able to perform the IO operations off the CPU as expected.

We’ll now explore some of the other flags. One important flag is -S, which determines how the system reacts when a process issues an I/O. With the flag set to SWITCH_ON_END, the system will NOT switch to another process while one is doing I/O, instead waiting until the process is completely finished. What happens when you run the following two processes, one doing I/O and the other doing CPU work? (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH_ON_END)
Kellen Kanarios2025623Answer The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED READY 1 3 BLOCKED READY 1 4 BLOCKED READY 1 5 BLOCKED READY 1 6 BLOCKED READY 1 7* RUN:io_done READY 1 8 DONE RUN:cpu 1 9 DONE RUN:cpu 1 10 DONE RUN:cpu 1 11 DONE RUN:cpu 1 As expected, it runs the IO process, blocking the CPU process until it is completed.

Now, run the same processes, but with the switching behavior set to switch to another process whenever one is WAITING for I/O (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH ON IO). What happens now? Use -c and -p to confirm that you are right

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: I assume that at timestep 1 we will still RUN:io. However, we will then switch the PID: 1 for the next 4 timesteps until it is completed. After one additional BLOCKED timestep, we will run RUN:io_done and complete both processes.
The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 3 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 4 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 5 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 6 BLOCKED DONE 1 7* RUN:io_done DONE 1

html:ol.html:li:
One other important behavior is what to do when an I/O completes. With -I IO RUN LATER, when an I/O completes, the process that issued it is not necessarily run right away; rather, whatever was running at the time keeps running. What happens when you run this combination of processes? (./process-run.py -l 3:0,5:100,5:100,5:100 -S SWITCH ON IO -I IO RUN LATER -c -p) Are system resources being effectively utilized?
Kellen Kanarios2025623Answer The resources are not efficiently utilized because we end up waiting until all of our CPU-bound processes finish before returning to the IO-bound process, which calls another IO:run and we do not get any concurrency benefit.
Kellen Kanarios2025623Solution The code output is pretty long but the end output is Stats: Total Time 31 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (67.74%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (48.39%)

Now run the same processes, but with -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE set, which immediately runs the process that issued the I/O. How does this behavior differ? Why might running a process that just completed an I/O again be a good idea?
Kellen Kanarios2025623Answer The behavior differs by returning to the IO-bound process, which can then immediately call the next IO:run before returning access to the CPU-bound processes, improving concurrency. In an attempt to predict the next IO call, it might be a good idea to run the process that just completed IO because it is more likely to make another IO call.
Kellen Kanarios2025623Solution The corresponding statistics are Stats: Total Time 21 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (100.00%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (71.43%)

Now run with some randomly generated processes, e.g., -s 1 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 2 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 3 -l 3:50,3:50. See if you can predict how the trace will turn out. What happens when you use -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE vs. -I IO RUN LATER? What hap- pens when you use -S SWITCH ON IO vs. -S SWITCH ON END?
Kellen Kanarios2025623Answer Since each instruction has a 50% chance of being CPU or I/O, I do not think there will be much benefit to IO_RUN_LATER vs. IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE. However, I believe it will be crucial to enable -S SWITCH_ON_IO over -S SWITCH_ON_END as the number of processes grow because we should always have waiting CPU instructions to run. In the case of two processes, we still may not improve that much because they will likely both be IO-bound at some point.
Kellen Kanarios2025623Solution For -s 1 (aka the seed), the observed performance between IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE and IO_RUN_LATER is the same. However, we do observe an improvement on both IO and CPU usage when we enable SWITCH_ON_IO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (3/66)
This was a very short chapter just introducing the actual API for some very important system calls. Namely

The fork system call allows a program to duplicate itself in a new process from the current instruction. This is quite literally a duplication which entails giving the child its own
Address space
Registers
Program counter
Importantly, you identify whether you are in the child or the parent via the return value of rc (0 for child), (PID of child for parent).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Which process is run first (the parent or the child) is non-deterministic and depends on the CPU scheduling algorithm.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Due to the remark, we need some way to enforce ordering among parent and child. This is the motivation for the wait system call. Namely, when the parent calls wait() it tells the CPU not to schedule it until the child completes.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: From the previous section, we saw the data structure for a process included the state. I assume wait() is implemented by adjusting the process state in the PCB somehow?

Doing a bit more research, it seems this is on the right track. Additionally, when a child completes it enters the ZOMBIE state, where it stores all the relevant information of the process, so that the parent process can then use this information. How this is actually coordinated seems to involve signals and wait_entries that essentially track all the children of a parent. The OS can then get a signal of completion from a child and find the corresponding parent?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: While fork() is useful in the sense that it allows us to spawn new processes from a parent process, it is still limited in that it can only execute instructions following the line of the fork() call in the parent process.

They briefly mention signals: what I expect to be an important topic in the future. Namely, you can send a signall to a process via kill() and the recipient program can "catch" this signal via the system call signal().
This naturally leads to the introduction of a user. The user determines which processes they are allowed to signal, where the superuser can arbitrarily signal all processes.
Deliberate-Practice (6/66).

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork(). Before calling fork(), have the main process access a variable (e.g., x) and set its value to something (e.g., 100). What value is the variable in the child process? What happens to the variable when both the child and parent change the value of x

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Both the parent and the child get a copy of the variable values i.e. x. When they modify x it only affects the copy of x in their local address space.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that opens a file (with the open() system call) and then calls fork() to create a new process. Can both the child and parent access the file descriptor returned by open()? What happens when they are writing to the file concurrently, i.e., at the same time?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Both do have access to the same file descriptor. I believe that they can interleave writes if writing at the same time but for me whichever process got ran first always finished writing prior the other starting. This may be a scheduling thing, where one process is able to complete before the other is scheduled?

html:ol.html:li: Write another program using fork(). The child process should print “hello”; the parent process should print “goodbye”. You should try to ensure that the child process always prints first; can you do this without calling wait() in the parent?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Using the previous problem, in the child, we can write something to a file. Then, in the parent, we can enforce an ordering by making the parent wait in a while loop until it reads the value written by the child. However, this is terribly inconvenient (and probably why wait() exists).

html:ol.html:li: Now write a program that uses wait() to wait for the child process to finish in the parent. What does wait() return? What happens if you use wait() in the child?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: wait() returns the pid of the child process that died. Using wait() in the child just immediately returns -1.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork() and then calls some form of exec() to run the program /bin/ls. See if you can try all of the variants of exec(), including execl(), execle(), execlp(), execv(), execvp(), and execvP(). Why do you think there are so many variants of the same basic call?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: It seems that some of them support different types of arguments i.e. execv family allows variable size argument list. Additionally, there is a distinction in the environment. This seems to be a list of environment variable values that exec{l,v}e() will search over. The p family use the same search as the shell i.e. the PATH.

html:ol.html:li: Write a slight modification of the previous program, this time us- ing waitpid() instead of wait(). When would waitpid() be useful?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If a parent process has multiple children, then wait() will only wait until the first one dies. waitpid() lets you specify waiting on a specific child process.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates a child process, and then in the child closes standard output (STDOUT_FILENO). What happens if the child calls printf() to print some output after closing the descriptor?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Nothing prints in the child process. However, stdout is still open in the parent process and able to print.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates two children, and connects the standard output of one to the standard input of the other, using the pipe() system call

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This required fflush() for me. I am not sure if this is true in general?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (7/66)
The contents of this chapter were revolved around two fundamental questions:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the first question, we introduce two modes user mode and kernel mode. In user mode, we are restricted with what sort of operations we can run.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: More importantly, in user mode, we are limited with what we can see. We will see later on in memory, but in user mode we have a limited address space allocated for the current process. However, in kernel mode, the address space is the entire computer and can therefore be very dangerous if we are not careful.
How do we switch from user and kernel mode without allowing processes to run instructions in kernel mode?
We must have some cooperation from the hardware. Namely, on boot, the OS code is run and sets up a trap table.

The hardware has some responsibility when switching to kernel mode. Namely, it must store two types of registers (1) user registers (2) kernel register.
The user registers are registers that contain the current program context i.e. stack variables, such as x = 1.
The kernel registers hold more meta-information, such as the PC, the syscall number of the calling syscall, etc.
Kellen Kanarios2025719Remark Each time we return to a process or switch between processes the hardware must perform a context switch. This is when they store all of the context of the current program before replacing it with the context of another program. The cost of such a switch is the tradeoff when performing scheduling of processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In order to implement the syscall, there needs to be some pre-agreed upon arrangement on where certain things go. For example, the syscall number must be stored in a pre-defined register. The general procedure (I think?) is as follows:

html:ol.html:li: syscall calls trap instruction defined by hardware.
hardware stores all information necessary to restore processes in pre-determined location.
hardware switches to kernel mode from user mode.
hardware jumps to code in OS to handle syscall (trap handler / interrupt handler?).
OS code checks pre-arranged register for the syscall number
OS executes corresponding syscall code.
OS restores program.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Due to relying on pre-determined registers, the syscalls are typically hand coded in assembly.
The OS must also perform checks regarding the safety of the requested operation i.e. we cannot let a process read the kernel code...
There is a similar mechanism for interrupts via an interrupt handler.
If the CPU is running a process, then how can the kernel decide to stop running it?
It can't... Instead the hardware performs period interrupts that trap to the OS code. The OS code can then decide what process to run via the scheduler (next section).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This section is a summary of my findings:

html:ol.html:li: Power signals reset of CPU state.
CPU runs BIOS, which checks that hardware works.
BIOS then runs bootloader (i.e. grub).
Bootloader places kernel code in right place and runs it.
Kernel then specifies things like trap table location (which is a special hardware instruction). Also must set timer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Toward the end of the previous section we alluded to the ominuous entity known as the scheduler. There is only one question that needs answering

html:div.html:em: How should we pick what process to run at any given time?

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
It turns out there is no right answer to this question (at least that is my takeaway from these three sections in the book). The reason for this is the conflicting priorities of various metrics. Namely,
turnaround time: the time to complete a process i.e. T_{\text {completion}} - T_{\text {arrival}}
fairness: this is computed according to some arbitrary index. However, the main idea is that worst case jobs should not be neglected
response time: how long it takes from issuing the command to it being scheduled.

FIFO: A naive and fair scheduler is to just serve each job in a first-in-first-out manner. However this suffers from the convoy effect. Namely, if we consider the case where the first job is very long. Then subsequent much shorter jobs will have much longer service times than they should.

SJF: To counteract this, we can try the shortest job first. If all jobs arrive at the same time, then SJF actually provably minimizes turnaround time. However, all jobs do not arrive at the same time and if the same problematic sequence for FIFO arrives sequentially it will also break SJF.

STCF: Up to this point, we have assumed that all jobs must be run to completion. However, we can relax this assumption and allow the OS to preempt a job (pausing it and starting a new job). With this, we introduce shortest to completion first, where we periodically preempt the current running job and then schedule the job with the shortest amount of time remaining until completion.

RR: While the previous algorithm is optimal with respect to turnaround time, it is not very good with respect to response time. In particular, long jobs may not be serviced for a very long time. The best we can do with respect to response time is just round robin, where we periodically randomly switch between processes. The intervals between switching are referred to as strides or time slices or quantum lengths.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Each time we switch between processes, we incur the cost of a context switch. Choosing larger time slices allows us to amortize this cost.

Prior to this, we had been ignoring I/O of the processes. A very simple way to not suffer substantial performance loss due to I/O is to simply divide the process up into its non I/O components and schedule them individually.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads does SJF deliver the same turnaround as FIFO?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For workloads that arrive in increasing order of total job time.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads and quantum lengths does SJF deliver the same response times as RR?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For shorter jobs and longer quantum lengths, RR and SJF should deliver similar response times.

html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with SJF as job lengths increase?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Response time degrades because the longest jobs have to wait for each of the shorter jobs to finish. If these shorter jobs become longer than the response time grows linearly(?).

html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with RR as quantum lengths increase? Can you write an equation that gives the worst-case response time, given N jobs?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The response time is the average over stride length number of preceding jobs. The formula can be written as \mathrm {RT} = \frac {1}{N} \sum _{i = 1}^{N} (i - 1)S = \frac {S(N - 1) N}{N} = S(N - 1)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In the previous section, we discussed algorithms that minimize turnaround time OR response time. Here, we will look at how to find a happy middle ground

The main idea is to maintain multiple queues with varying levels of priority. Each queue will then be scheduled round-robin within priority groups and lower priorities will not be scheduled until all processes of higher priority are completed. This introduces a few problems that we must remedy

One approach is the idea of allotment. Essentially, each process is given an alloted amount of time at each priority. Once the process has run for the alloted amount of time at a given priority it moves down to the next priority.

Clearly, this achieves a good response time because it essentially reduces to round robin. This also does not tank performance because, if we recall that STCF is the optimal algorithm for turnaround time, we see that short jobs will still finish relatively quickly because they will spend all of their time at high priority (assuming a high enough allotment).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: We also saw in the previous section that one way to handle I/O is by breaking it into multiple jobs. However, this can result in gaming an allotment scheduler by I/O-ing right before you reach allotment essentially ensuring your process always remains the highest priority. To deal with this, we can count the total time spent by each of the jobs toward the allotment.

html:div.html:em: How do we prevent starvation of lower priority queues?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems the easiest way for this is to just periodically move all processes back to the highest priority.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The key idea here is rather than maintain queues of priorities we instead assign each job a certain proportion of the CPU time. The obvious question:
How do we ensure the jobs actually match the desired proportion?
The first approach is the concept of ticket currency. The idea is to give each user a finite amount of tickets and have them allocate them amongs their processes.
2025726Remark They also allow ticket transfer and ticket inflation, where a process can transfer tickets i.e. client / server, or temporarily increase their number of tickets.
2025726Remark The benefit of the ticket currency approach is the simplicity of the implementation. You simply draw a random number in the range of the total number of tickets and select the process with that number.
2025726Remark The natural question of how to actually assign tickets is very difficult and not solved.
Deterministic version: The ambitious observer might wonder if there is a deterministic algorithm to achieve the same effect. Luckily, the answer is actually yes! Rather than randomly sample tickets, we set the stride to be inversely proportional to the ticket count. We then track the pass (a running sum for each process that is incremented by the processes stride). To formalize-ish,
Choose process with lowest pass.
Increment the chosen process pass by the process stride.
Repeat. We see that a low stride (high ticket count) results in being scheduled more frequently.
2025726Remark There is still an advantage of lottery-based scheduling: no global state. If new processes join, it is unclear what to set their initial pass at.
2025726Remark On linux, the user can manually set priorities using nice and setting the niceness of a process in [-20, 20).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We are now moving on from scheduling into the wonderful world of all things memory.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
If we think about what we have done so far, we have an assortment of processes that are constantly being switched between. However, each of these process has their own assortment of register states, but more importantly they each have their own state in memory.
For register states, the OS has its own reserved memory, where it can easily store and switch between register states.
For memory, when we switch processes, are we expected to clear out memory for the next process by storing to disk? NO! This motivates the idea behind address spaces. Namely, each process is given its own address space (chunk in memory) to use at its own discretion. This will then ensure that other processes do not overwrite the program state of one another. The address space of a process typically consists of three components
Code of the program
Stack: local var, arguments, return values
Heap: dynamically allocated memory
2025729Remark As we will see later, every address we see is actually virtual, meaning it is not the location in physical memory.
API: We only really need two methods, a way to get memory and a way to release memory. These are implemented via malloc and free. However, the use of these is usually accompanied with a handful of common errors:
Forget to initialize mem.
Forget to free mem.
Freeing memory early: dangling pointer.
Freeing the same memory twice: double free.
Passing something not allocated earlier: invalid free.
2025729Remark When a process dies, the OS reclaims all of that process memory. This means that as long as your program memory is bounded, leakage will not do any sort of catastrophic damage. However, it is still not good practice.
2025729Remark malloc and free are not syscalls. The heap is pre-allocated with some allotment of memory. If the program exceeds this memory then malloc will call a syscall brk / sbrk to increment / decrement heap mem pointer.
2025729Remark You can actually initialize and use your own heap-like memory through mmap. This memory is not associated with any particular file and is an anonymous memory region within your program.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The next question is once each program has their own address space, how do we unify the experience for the user.

html:ol.html:li: We want every process to have the illusion that is starts at address 0
We want every process to have the illusion of a large address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, we also do not want to give up the protection / speed of the address space abstraction. The overarching idea is virualizing the address space. This means that the address the user sees is not the true address in physical memory. How we achieve this will be a longstanding topic for the next few entries of these notes.

Base and Bounds: The first approach is base and bounds. For this approach, the hardware has a dedicated base and bound register to indicate where the address space for a given process begins and ends. It is the OSes job to ensure that these registers are filled with the correct values for the current process.
The hardware must provide protected instructions to allow the OS to modify the base and bound registers.

An address translation is then performed by the hardware by adding the base to the virtual address and checking it remains within the bounds. This is done by the memory management unit (MMU). A common theme we will see is that hardware support is crucial to achieve good virtualization.

html:div.html:em: to support a large addres space with base and bounds we suffer significant internal fragmentation i.e. in between the base and bound there is a lot of unused memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To better understand why fragmentation is such an issue: if we have 16kb total memory and each process requests 4kb but only uses 2kb, then we can only maintain 4 processes despite having the memory to service 8! To attempt to remedy this, we will introduce the idea of segmentation.

Instead of pre-allocating the memory between the heap and stack, why don't we allocate on demand. To do so, we can treat the code, heap, and stack as their own segments with their own base and bound registers. An address could then be decomposed as \underbrace {0 1}_{\text {segment}}\underbrace {0 1 \cdots 11}_{\text {offset}} This also enables an idea of memory sharing, where process can share a portion of memory (such as the code) to save memory on the system.
Need to add a protection bit to indicate whether a process can modify certain addrs.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This limits flexibility in the sense that we have no control over where the middle region was allocated and therefore we cannot grow beyond the initial bound.
The hardware also has to support the stack growing backwards when it checks the bounds. To do so, it has to perform an additional check based on the segment number.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
We have been talking a lot about how to abstract physical address to provide ease of use for the programmer. However, we have yet to discuss how to actually allocate these physical addresses in the first place. When allocating memory, there are a few issues we need to be careful about
External fragmentation: lots of small unallocated non-contiguous chunks of memory.
Internal fragmentation: allocated memory that is unused in the process it is allocated for. To start, we make a few simplifying assumptions:
After memory has been allocated, it cannot be moved.
The size of memory is fixed.
The user does not specify the size in free. First, we can quickly deal with the last problem by introducing a header. The OS stores additional metadata, such as the size of the allocated memory in a header directly preceding the returned pointer in memory.
If int* x = malloc(sizeof(int)) then header = (header_t)* x - 1.
OS needs to find memory for the requested memory + size of the header.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The general allocation algorithm consists of maintaining some free-list that contains the address and length of free memory. An allocation is done by some policy over this list. When memory is re-claimed, the list is searched for adjacent memory and if it is found then it is coalesced into a larger chunk of free memory.
Best fit: find smallest chunk that is big enough
leaves lots of small chunks
Worst fit: find largest chunk Both of these require searching the entire list, so instead we can introduce some heuristics.
First fit: find first block that can service.
Next fit: find first block that can service starting from the previous allocation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Outside of the allocation policy, we can make data-structure level optimization. One such optimization is segmentation lists. The idea is to maintain different lists that correspond to different commonly allocated object types.

html:ul.html:li: Grow list by requesting segments as multiple size of the objects.
Can return to main list when no references.
Can also leave objects pre-iitialized on list to eliminate calls to constructor / destructor.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Assume memory is size 2^n. Search through memory to find smallest block that can service request.

When block is freed,
Check if the buddy is free.
If the buddy is free.
Coalesce into larger block
Repeat. Can easily check buddy because address differ by one bit at each level. Just modify the corresponding bit and check the free data structure (bitmap?) if the address is free. If it is then move up and check the next bit.

Previously, we have only dealt with memory allocations as variable sized chunks i.e. the amount of memory allocated can be anything. A simple idea to avoid the drastic fragmentation this can induce is to instead divide memory into fixed size chunks. This idea is called paging, where each chunk is a page.

html:div.html:em: how do we virtualize memory with pages?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Problem 1: The page table can get ridiculously large.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For now, we consider a linear page table (an array with entry for each VPN). If we support a 32 bit address space with 4kb pages and each entry in the page table is 4 bytes then this is 2^{32} / 2^{12} * 4 = 2^{22} bytes of memory for the page table!

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Before, we relied on the MMU to translate between physical and virtual addresses via simple operations like base and bound. However, there is no way the MMU could support this many arbitrary translations. Therefore, the page table itself must be stored in physical memory.

In addition to the address translation, the page table also keeps track of the following bits:
Valid bit: whether this particular VPN has memory allocated to it i.e. mem between stack / heap does not need to be allocated.
Protection bit: determines whether the calling program can read / write / execute.
Present bit: whether the page currently resides in physical memory.
Dirty bit: has the page been modified since being paged in.
Reference bit: has the page been accessed (used for eviction policy).

Problem 2: Every time we want to perform an address translation (very often) we must
Compute VPN from address
Access page table entry in physical memory (slow)
Compute address with offset + PPN.
Access corresponding physical page in physical memory. This is very slow!
Due to the previously discussed issues, we need a faster way of performing address translation to make paging a feasible option. A common theme: we will throw hardware at it

Specifically, we will add an additional cache known as the translation lookaside buffer that caches address translations. Generally, an address translation proceeds as:
Extract VPN
Check if VPN in TLB
If in TLB: get translation
Otherwise: TLB miss -> trap to OS (RISC)

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: We have to be a little careful. The OS code itself is located in memory and is therefore subject to our paging system. We can imagine that on TLB miss the hardware tries to load the TLB miss handler code by checking the TLB, getting the translation, and loading the code. But what if the translation for the TLB miss handler is not in the TLB?!?! To not worry about these things the TLB miss handler gets a permanent spot in physical memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Formally, an entry in the TLB consists of
VPN
PPN
Other bits
Valid bit: has valid translation (different from PTE valid bit) can be invalid on initialization, eviction, or context switch.
Protection bit: whether the requested access can be performed.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Each process has different address translations corresponding to the same VPN.
Keep additional address space identifier (ASID) in TLB entry. We also need an ASID register that we set at every process switch to identify the current running process.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: "Ram is not always ram". Referring to the fact that a physical memory access takes a substantial different amount of time depending on whether it hit in the TLB.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In a real software managed TLB, the TLB consisted of the following components:
Global bits for globally shared pages,
8 bit ASID,
Coherence bits for cache-coherence,
32-64 TLB entries,
A wired register to indicate how many slots to reserve in the TLB for things like TLB miss handler.

Another important tidbit: when using very fast caches, even an access to the TLB is a CPU bottleneck (accessing TLB is longer than accessing cache). To get around this, there are virtually indexed caches that circumvent the need for performing an address translation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As previously discussed, as it currently stands, the page table takes up too much memory.
Solution 1: Just use bigger pages! Unfortunately, this will suffer from severe internal fragmentation (processes not using their allocated memory) and so we would prefer to avoid this solution.

Solution 2: Paging + segmentation. Instead of a page table for all of memory, keep 3 page tables: one for code, stack, and heap. This only partially resolves the problem.
Same problems as segmentation. Variable sized page tables will cause external fragmentation
We can do better. Within each heap, there is still a (smaller) version of the same problem: a bunch of unused allocated PTEs.
Multi-level page tables: Finally, we have arrived to the idea of a multi-level page table. The idea can be summarized as

html:ol.html:li: Chop page table up into pages.
Do not allocated pages with no valid references (i.e. none of the corresponding are being used).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This has a few advantages:

html:ol.html:li: Fits easily in memory. Allocate one page when an entry is used.
Proportional to the amount of address space actually in use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The obvious downside is the added complexity. Namely, on every TLB miss we must make two loads of memory (1) the page directory entry and (2) the page table entry.

fr:title.text: What happens when memory is full?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What happens when memory is full?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We reserve space on disk for pages that cannot fit in physical memory. This is referred to as swap space.

We need to add an additional present bit to the PTE. On a TLB miss, the hardware (or software) will check the page table and see the present bit to indicate whether the page is in physical memory. If not, we must trap to the OS and this is known as a page fault.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The hardware never handles page faults because this would require understanding swap space + knowing how to perform I/O with disk.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Three important cases to consider:

html:ol.html:li: Page is present and valid.

html:ul.html:li: Hardware can just grab physical address from PTE.

html:ol.html:li: Page is not present but valid.

html:ul.html:li: Hardware must call page fault handler to retrieve page.

html:ol.html:li: Page is not valid.

html:ul.html:li: Throw exception and likely terminate process as it is accessing invalid memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Up to this point, we have reduced our focus to assuming physical memory was sufficiently large memory. In practice, this is rarely the case. To complete our discussion on memory, we must lift this assumption.
In order to handle overflowing memory, we must reserve some space on physical disk to store the additional pages. This space is referred to as swap space.

To keep track of such memory, the page table entry instead contains a disk address. The OS knows what to do because of the additional present bit indicating whether the page is in physical memory. If not, we must trap to the OS and this is known as a page fault.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The hardware never handles page faults because this would require understanding swap space + knowing how to perform I/O with disk.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Three important cases to consider:

html:ol.html:li: Page is present and valid.

html:ul.html:li: Hardware can just grab physical address from PTE.

html:ol.html:li: Page is not present but valid.

html:ul.html:li: Hardware must call page fault handler to retrieve page.

html:ol.html:li: Page is not valid.

html:ul.html:li: Throw exception and likely terminate process as it is accessing invalid memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now that we know we must occasionally page out some pages from physical memory to swap spaces. The large question remains:

html:div.html:em: What page do you evict at any given time?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Such a decision is a policy, and the quality of a policy is often weighed by the average mean access time (AMAT) given by AMAT = (1 - P_{miss})T_P + P_{miss} T_D where T_P is the time to access physical memory and T_D is time to access disk. For large discrepancies in T_M and T_D, we can have a substantial difference in AMAT for even very low P_{miss}.
The "optimal" policy is one that evicts the page whose next use is farthest away. Unfortunately, this is not feasible to implement as we do not know the future.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A desirable and surprisingly not guaranteed property we want in our policy is the stack property, which guarantess that if the cache size increases our miss rate gets no worse. This is not the case with FIFO and is known as Belady's anomaly.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We instead consider the least recently used (LRU) heuristic. The effectiveness of such a policy can be attributed to the principle of locality. Namely, in this case, we enforce temporal locality, where resident pages are close in terms of time accessed.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In practice, we do not implement pure LRU either. This would require maintaining a separate data structure and modifying at every memory access. Instead, we utilize the clock algorithm, where a reference bit is set to 1 each time an access occurs. The algorithm then proceeds by looping through all pages
If reference bit is 1, set to 0 and proceed to next page.
If reference bit is 0 evict the current page.
LRU is by no means optimal and in the looping workload (where we loop access one more page than the page table size) we miss 100 percent of the time.
What happens when you dereference a nullptr? First, we check the TLB for the VPN 0. We miss and then check the page table. The page will be marked invalid, and we will trap to the OS.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
LRU is a global policy, meaning that it does not distinguish the memory use by process. Therefore, memory hog processes can hog memory. To deal with this, one approach is segmented FIFO, where they maintain a FIFO queue for each process. If the queue exceeds the resident set size then the first-in page is evicted. They maintain two lists: clean page free list and dirty page list.
If a page is evicted but free it is put on the clean page free list.
Otherwise, it is put on the dirty page list. If the original process faults on a page still on the dirty page list it reclaims that page and avoids the I/O.

html:ul.html:li: When heap memory is allocated a lot of it goes unused.
Do not need to zero allocated memory until it is used
We do not need to copy pages across address spaces unless they are modified.
i.e. fork(), shared libraries, etc.
Both VPNs map to same PPN.
PTE has additional bits to trap to OS on write.
Cluster small page I/O together and perform one write all at once.
Collect large batch from global dirty list.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A common convention for ease of implementation is also to include the kernel in each process address space. This can be made even more efficiently via copy on write, where each process will just refer to the same kernel code in physical memory.
Each thread contains its own program counter (PC) and a private set of registers. They do NOT contain their own address space like a process.
Similar to the process control block (PCB), in order to perform a context switch each process needs a thread control block(TCB).
Additionally, the process needs one stack per-thread. This does ruin our neatly partitioned address space. However, it is generally OK because each individual stack is not too large.

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: within a process, there are often highly parallelizable components. However, we do not want to have to divide the program into processes and perform the much more expensive context switch.
Avoid blocking I/O: even if you do not want to parallelize your program you can still use a thread to avoid blocking I/O by just having that thread blocked while you continue doing other work.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Suppose we have the following sequence of instructions (pseudo-assembly) mov addr reg add 1 reg mov reg addr If you have two threads running this and hope the end result is that you increment the corresponding variable by two, then you might be in for some disappointment. This is a non-atomic operation because conceivably
thread 1 could store the value of the register
thread 2 could store the value of the register
thread 1 increments
thread 2 increments
end result is only 1 increment This is a race condition and code that is accessed by multiple threads is the critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while(lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1

html:ul.html:li: Still have O(n) coherence traffic on release.
Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set next slot to "go-ahead"
Constant coherence traffic.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: O(n) memory for the locks. Padding to 64b can get expensive.

html:ul.html:li: Lock is a pointer to a node
Each thread has own node
html:ul.html:pre: acquire(lock): I->next = null; predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock,I) if predecessor != nil //some node holds lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I //predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait //spin till lock is free release(lock): if (I->next == null) //no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock,I,nil) //make sure… return //CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next = nil //spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false //wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
global (shared) count : integer := P
procedure central_barrier if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P else repeat until count == P

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: When count == p one thread can reach the next barrier and decrement count again. Then all other threads will be stuck.

Each thread gets local "sense" variable. global (shared) count : integer := P global (shared) sense : bool := true global (private) local_sense : bool = false
procedure central_barrier local_sense = !local_sense if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P sense = local_sense else repeat until sense == local_sense
